elements.sent-2411-001: We present an expressive logic over trace formulas, based on binary state predicates, chop, and least fixed-points, for precise specification of programs with recursive procedures.
elements.sent-2411-002: Both, programs and trace formulas, are equipped with a direct-style, fully compositional, denotational semantics that on programs coincides with the standard SOS of recursive programs.
elements.sent-2411-003: We design a compositional proof calculus for proving finite-trace program properties, and prove soundness as well as (relative) completeness.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that each program can be mapped to a semantics-preserving trace formula and, vice versa, each trace formula can be mapped to a canonical program over slightly extended programs, resulting in a Galois connection between programs and formulas.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results shed light on the correspondence between programming constructs and logical connectives.
elements.sent-2411-006: It is uncommon that specification languages used in program verification are as expressive as the programs they are intended to specify: In model checking \cite{CGP99 one typically abstracts away from data and unwinds unbounded control structures.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Specification of unbounded computations is achieved by recursively defined temporal operators.
elements.sent-2411-008: In deductive verification \cite{HaehnleHuisman19 , first-order Hoare-style contracts \cite{Hoare71 are the basis of widely used specification languages \cite{acsl,JML-Ref-Manual .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The latter specify computation by taking symbolic memory snapshots in terms of first-order formulas, typically at the beginning and at the end of unbounded control structures, such as procedure call and return, or upon loop entry and exit.
elements.sent-2411-010: Contracts permit to approximate the effect of unbounded computation as a finite number of first-order formulas against which a program's behavior can be verified.
elements.sent-2411-011: Imagine, in contrast, a logic for program specification that is at least as expressive as the programs it is supposed to describe.
elements.sent-2411-012: Formulas $\phi$ of such a logic characterize a generally infinite set of program computation traces $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-013: One can then form judgments of program statements $S$ of the form $\judge{S {\phi}$ with the natural semantics that any possible trace $\sigma$ of $S$ is one of the traces described by $\phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Two arguments against such a trace logic are easily raised: first, one expects a specification language to be capable of abstraction from implementation details; second, its computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-015: Regarding the first, we note that any desired degree of abstraction can be achieved by definable constructs, i.e.~syntactic sugar, in a sufficiently rich trace logic.
elements.sent-2411-016: Regarding the second, it is far from obvious whether the computational worst-case tends to manifest itself in practical verification \cite{HaehnleHuisman19 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: First experiments seem to indicate this is not the case.
elements.sent-2411-018: On the other hand, a rich, trace-based specification logic bears many advantages:.
elements.sent-2411-019: On the practical side, trace-based specification permits to express, for example, that an event or a call happened (exactly once, at least once) or that did not happen.
elements.sent-2411-020: Likewise, one can specify without reference to the target code (via assertions) that a condition holds at some point which is not necessarily an endpoint of a procedure call.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is also possible to specify inter-procedural properties, such as call sequences, absence of callbacks, etc. Such properties are important in security analysis and they are difficult or impossible to express with Hoare-style contracts or in temporal logic.
elements.sent-2411-022: The semantics of trace formulas in terms of trace sets can be closely aligned with a suitable program semantics, which greatly simplifies soundness and completeness proofs.
elements.sent-2411-023: An expressive trace logic makes for simple and natural completeness proofs for judgments $\judge{S {\phi}$, because it is unnecessary to encode the program semantics in order to construct sufficiently strong first-order conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In a calculus for judgments of the form $\judge{S {\phi}$ one one can design inference rules that directly decompose judgments into simpler ones, but also algebraic rules that simplify and transform $\phi$, and one may mix both reasoning styles.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The semantics of programs, of trace formulas, and the rules of the calculus can be formulated in a fully compositional manner: definitions and inference rules involve no intermediate state or context.
elements.sent-2411-026: In consequence, we are able to establish a close correspondence between programs and formulas, which sheds light on the exact relation between each program construct and its corresponding logical connective.
elements.sent-2411-027: We formulate and prove a Galois connection that formalizes this fact.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our main contribution is a trace logic for imperative programs with recursive procedures where we formalize and prove the claims above.
elements.sent-2411-029: We restrict attention to the terminating executions of programs, i.e.~to their finite trace semantics.
elements.sent-2411-030: This is not a fundamental limitation, but the desire not to obscure the construction with complications that may be added later.
elements.sent-2411-031: Still, adaptating the theory to maximal traces (including infinite runs) is not trivial, and working out the details is left as future work (see Section~\ref{sec:non-term-progr ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our paper is structured as follows: In Section~\ref{sec:rec we define the programming language \Rec\ used throughout this paper and we give it a standard SOS semantics \cite{Plotkin04 .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section~\ref{sec:rec-traces we define a denotational semantics for \Rec\ in fully compositional style and prove it equivalent to the SOS semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section~\ref{sec:logic-traces we introduce our trace logic and map programs to formulas by the concept of a strongest trace formula, which is shown to fully preserve program semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In Section \ref{sec:proof-calculus we define a proof calculus for judgments and show it to be sound and complete.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In Section \ref{sec:canonical-programs , using the concept of a canonical program, we map trace formulas back to programs in the slightly extended language $\Rec^\ast$ with non-deterministic guards.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We prove that $\Rec^\ast$ and trace formulas form a Galois connection via strongest trace formulas and canonical programs.
elements.sent-2411-038: In Section~\ref{sec:related-work we discuss related work, in Section~\ref{sec:future-work we sketch some extensions, including options to render specifications more abstract and how to prove consequence among trace formulas.}}
